questions.question: A company's development team is measured on the number of features shipped per sprint, while the operations team is measured on system uptime and incident-free weeks. A developer pushes a large release that causes a server outage at 2 a.m. — Ops gets paged, Dev does not. According to the lecture, what is the ROOT cause of this conflict?
options: The developers wrote poor-quality code that Ops should have rejected earlier.
The two teams have conflicting incentives built into how their jobs are measured, not a failure of individual people.
The operations team lacks the technical skills to handle complex deployments.
The company should simply hire more developers so releases are smaller and safer.
questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly names 'conflicting incentives' (Problem 2) as the structural cause. Dev is rewarded for shipping more, faster; Ops is rewarded for stability. When pain from failure lands only on Ops, Ops resists change — not because Ops is wrong, but because the structure sets them against each other. Option A blames individuals rather than structure. Option C misidentifies the problem as a skills gap. Option D addresses team size, not the incentive structure.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST captures the official definition of DevOps according to the lecture?
options: A set of automation tools (Docker, Jenkins, Kubernetes) that replace manual server operations.
A dedicated team of engineers responsible for building and running the CI/CD pipeline.
A combination of cultural philosophies, practices, and tools for delivering software quickly and reliably.
A project management methodology that merges the development and operations departments into one.
questions.explanation: The lecture quotes the official definition verbatim: 'DevOps is a combination of cultural philosophies, practices, and tools.' It then stresses that culture is listed first deliberately. Option A reduces DevOps to tools only — the lecture warns that buying tools while keeping the wall means you 'just have expensive software.' Option B describes the DevOps Engineer role, not the discipline itself. Option D describes a structural org change, which the lecture never prescribes as the definition.

questions.question: The lecture describes the DevOps delivery loop with an infinity symbol as its unofficial logo. What concept does the infinity symbol MOST directly represent?
options: DevOps work never finishes because software always has bugs that must be fixed forever.
The delivery cycle — plan, code, test, build, deploy, operate, monitor — repeats continuously as the product evolves.
DevOps engineers must be on call 24/7 with no end to their working hours.
There are infinitely many DevOps tools available, and teams must keep learning new ones.
questions.explanation: The infinity symbol represents the continuous, repeating delivery loop: every improvement (new feature, bug fix, performance change) travels the same path again — plan → code → test → build → deploy → operate → monitor → back to plan. The loop never ends because successful software always needs more work. Options A, C, and D all take 'infinity' as a metaphor for something else (bugs, working hours, tools), none of which the lecture introduces in this context.

questions.question: A startup has purchased the full DevOps tool stack — containerization, a CI/CD platform, infrastructure-as-code, and monitoring. However, the Dev and Ops teams still have separate backlogs, separate on-call rotations, and do not share post-incident reviews. Based on the lecture, how should this situation be characterized?
options: This is a valid DevOps implementation; tools are the hard part, and culture will follow naturally once teams see the automation working.
The startup has DevOps at the infrastructure level but still needs to add security tooling (DevSecOps) before it qualifies.
The wall between Dev and Ops still exists; having the tools without the culture means the company does not yet have DevOps.
This is fine as long as the CI/CD pipeline deploys to production without manual approval gates.
questions.explanation: The lecture states directly: 'If you buy all the tools but keep the wall, you don't have DevOps; you just have expensive software.' The three core values — fast delivery, stability, and collaboration — require shared ownership and shared responsibility. Separate backlogs, separate on-call, and no shared post-mortems are all symptoms of the wall still standing. Option A inverts the priority (culture comes first, per the official definition). Option B introduces a DevSecOps requirement that is irrelevant here. Option D focuses only on deployment gates, ignoring the broader cultural separation.

questions.question: According to the lecture, how do DevOps and SRE (Site Reliability Engineering) relate to each other?
options: They are competing methodologies: DevOps prioritizes speed while SRE prioritizes reliability, so teams must choose one.
SRE is one specific way of putting DevOps principles into practice, with extra emphasis on reliability; both aim at fast AND reliable delivery.
SRE replaced DevOps in large-scale companies because it provides a more rigorous engineering framework.
DevOps is a subset of SRE: all DevOps practices are included in SRE, but SRE also adds formal error budgets and service-level objectives.
questions.explanation: The lecture describes DevOps and SRE as 'two sides of the same coin — both aimed at fast and reliable delivery,' and calls SRE 'one specific way of putting DevOps principles into practice.' Neither replaces the other, and they are not in opposition. Option A incorrectly frames them as competing trade-offs. Option C claims SRE 'replaced' DevOps, which the lecture does not say. Option D reverses the relationship — the lecture does not say DevOps is a subset of SRE, and it does not mention error budgets or SLOs in this introductory lecture.
